speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This conversation with Helmer Ring Sage took place at his home in Moscow on February 27th, 1976. The interviewer is Sam Schrager.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And the crop footage spoiled over there. And I would have been sunk worse over there than I would have been, than I did get over here. Here I only owed a thousand to the dairy bank and I had forty -four head of cattle to ox that. They got their money's worth of, could have, if they could have sold them. Thirty -two, nineteen thirty -two men a week went down to two bits over there to park. And the only man that saved me was old President Roosevelt. Delano Roosevelt.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: He made the stipulation that any family man that had, regardless, if he was a fireman and had fifty, he was exempt fifty -five acres of crop for his family.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: No mortgage could take it. So that's what I done. I had a little oats, I don't know, thirty -two dollars worth probably is all I had.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I sold pocketed of money every time I come in.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Now, Old Harsh was a nice banker. He's the one that I didn't want to borrow that much money to start with. I only wanted five hundred to buy me. I was going to, here was the deal, I didn't tell you.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I'd bought a state land on terms. Eighty acres right along there in park, there close to this Nolan place. That's, well, it'd be west of where Jellibear is, up in the mountain there. Just about as soon as you enter into park, get over the top of the grate. Well anyway, I got that fourteen hundred dollars from my brother Simon, one died with cancer. He lent me the cash to make the down payment. And then I got, when I got the fireman on the ridge, you see, the first two years I was making pretty good, some of it. One year, I mean, I made good.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I raised some beans on forty -four acres of ground Dad had.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And I got thirteen hundred pounds to the acre.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And I come putting in missing it like some of them. One of the neighbors there, he tried to hold me, he said, oh you don't sell yet.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Said the retail in Spokane, they tell me I haven't sent. Well I could have gotten nine cents.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I was back a little bit. I could have gotten nine cents. But old Guernsey, I had the hall clear on the other side then.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Old Guernsey, he says, you want to sell this morning ring to Hayden?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I said, well I don't know.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I said, what's the price?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: He says, nine cents. He's laughing, kind of, you know. Him and McGill was in part, you see. I thought, God damn that smile, I thought maybe he had something up his sleeve I could get a little more.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I said, how about a dime?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: No, he said, or a dime, he said, pretty good. Well it was.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I was a damn fool not taking it, see.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Well I held that dog dog crop. I said, pretty soon this rice here man, come down here, he want this money. He wanted it right now. I said, okay, I'll call up McGill and I wasn't going to fight him. He come and tried when I asked him to. He wanted his money, by God he had the right to get it.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: He said he had to pay his crew, which was nothing more than right.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How long after the crop was in, was this?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Oh, that was after I started hauling, I had it hauled down to the warehouse.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Wasn't very long after he thrashed it?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Well, it may be, no not too long, I don't know, maybe it was a month or two, I can't remember that for sure. But anyway, he, I called up McGill.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I said, how much is beans?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: He kind of was a squeaky voice guy. He said, beans are coming down, there's still seven and a half cents in the dirt, you better sell. I said, well, I'll sell my share, but my dad had told me not to sell his share.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Till I let him know. Now he was a dang fool for not letting McGill know when the price, and he could have told McGill here, when that price is suitable, sell. Well, he should have done it.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: He didn't do it.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: By the time I wrote to Dad and he finally fiddled around like some of the rest of the farmers to spring, he did get, he left it to McGill then and he got five cents, the rest of the farmers, beans laid in the warehouse.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: They couldn't sell them. I was dang lucky, I got that seven and a half.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Your father sold for five?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: He sold for five, his third share.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But the other farmers couldn't sell at all?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And I sold mine in the fall, you see, for the seven and a half.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The other farmers didn't sell at all?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: No, a lot of them didn't sell. They was vacillating. This one guy, Dan Gold, that brother's the one that trashed for us. By God, he was the one that told me about prices so good in Spokane. Fuck God, they were retailing in hell, you wouldn't go about that. Little damn farmers, these grain buyers, boy, you better listen to them. I got my lesson there. And then I went as far as one year then, by gosh, if I didn't even borrow a dollar and a half on my week.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Week was a dollar and a half, I should have sold. No, I had the damn thing till the next year.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: You know what I got?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I got the shit, the warehouse took it for storage, 84 cents.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: That's what put me under here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It cost 84 cents for storage?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: No, yeah, it modded to that.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: It modded to that.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Well, that isn't counting what I borrowed. I borrowed a dollar and a half, I had to pay up.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Don't you see?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: That dollar and a half I borrowed.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Dollar and a half a bushel? Yeah, I borrowed, you see, that would be 85, that would be, what?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: That would be 50, 65 cents extra that I overdrew, you see, that I owed the bank.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What year was that, do you know? Oh, God, there's about 19, wait now, I can put in your figure, that'll be, what year would that be?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: This was 1919 when I had the good beans.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And, let's see now, 1919.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: No, wait, I had the beans.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: You see, the way I keep track is, my oldest daughter, she was born in 1918, and she was a year old. Dad was working with the bean horse, my other boys. And that year old she would, that'd be 1919, I had the good crop of beans that I sold. And I got $2 ,100 for my share at seven and a half cents. Now he could have got a thousand dollars for yours. No, he didn't, because he only got five cents, you see, so he lacked, oh, a couple, three hundred dollars of getting what he should have gotten. Well, in a way, I got this done, and this here,

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: this whole idea, this idea of speculation, do you think if you look back at it, did you do better to sell right away?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I did, but God, listen, then after that I did.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Then what did God tell you then?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I think it was 19, no, just 1919 I sold, I mean I had the big bean crop and then you're putting that on here.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Are you?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Oh yeah, I started doing it, I started doing it on a crop. Well, it's only on a body. Well, if you can straighten it out. Sure. In a way, I sold 1919, a bean crop I sold, 1920 or 21, I can't remember for sure, that I got two and a quarter for the wheat, but I didn't have much yield.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I only had 16 bushels of that market spring, you see, but it was clean.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: The old crop at the warehouse on this side was the man that gave me the big offer.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I was coming down there, you know, he said, How much of this wheat you got, Green Sage?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I said, I've got 1019 sacks, counting the old man's share.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: He gets a third of the wheat, see. Well, he said,

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I understand, well, wheat had been going 2, 12, 12, 14, 16 long there. You told me this.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I think you told me about this. Because you got good money. So then, when it went, he said, now, he said, it's been jiggling up and down.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: He says, how will 220 strike you?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Fine, I said, make it more if you want to. And, no.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Let's see, wait a minute, I've got that mixed up.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: 220, what's that?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Yes, boy, gosh, a good boy.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: 220. And the next day I come down, he says, I understand the other warehouses give him a little more.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: He says, oh, yeah, that's when he said, how 2 and a quarter strike you?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I said, make it 2, then. And so, that year, I got good money out of the wheat. 5 ,100 dollars. But dad had to have 1 ,700 of that, you see. But that still didn't put me out of that. Then I got fed up with wheat. I happened to have one sow in a half -maker lot.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: It used to be the old orchard. I planted it for pasture, but we didn't have a little sow for them. And I had a little barley.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I ground the barley, the roll that fed to this sow, and just crowded them. I wanted to see what I could do. And by golly, in five months I had 200 -pound pigs. And I got to figuring on that. I said, Jesus, that peach wheat's all two pieces. That was... I beat the price for raising wheat on that little lot of ground, see. And it counted the grain that I'd had in oil. I said, man, I can just go into hogs if I can get a building. Well, first year I didn't. First year I used dad's... He had a big machine shed.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And I made mistakes. Two mistakes.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: One was, I had to... You know, where the sows were pinned off together, partitioned off. Each sow had one sow that would eat this other sow's pigs if he hatched. She cleaned up one sow's pig or a damn near before I caught on.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Well, another mistake I made that year was... We used to earn cattle the first year I was there. We had cattle. We sold them the whole... Quick, we couldn't like that. We couldn't get no person. So I said...

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I did...

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Now, it's salt. I put in this big cattle trough, see. Rock salt.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And melted. And the pigs would come and drink that.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And they died off like flies. You went down to the water... Down to the pond, right.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: They filled up and busted it. I didn't know what was going on. Nice, fat pigs. Just from that salt.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: What the hell?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: One of the neighbors come along there and he says... Helmer, he says, I'll tell you what's the matter.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I says, what?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Said, we had a sow. We fed some ice cream salt. Brian. Said, an hour or two after she drank that, she bloated and died. He says, here's your trouble. He says, you got this salt trough here. He said, if you get a block salt, he said it won't happen. Boy, that's when I got busy.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I only had 17 pigs left out of 85 little pigs that first year. I mean, that's the second year after I'd raised it once out. Then I got busy. Boy, that's no good. I wrote to my dad if he'd let me move a house he had on the back field, that scouting place where I had the big bean crop.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I said, could I move that over?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: He wanted to know how I could do it. I said, I can do it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did you do that?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: He started to tell you. I done it. I done it. I got a stump puller down. This poor little friend of mine that died last spring, Chuck Graham. His dad had a big Hercules stump puller about this high. They were all good. Them days, you know. His dad run a little sawmill up in the canyon there by Peck.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Besides, he had a gristmill in Peck where I could get my flower ground.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: He was a nice old man. I said, I'll finish that later. He says, go ahead and use it. I took it up there and barred another neighbor west. 200 foot cable. That's 400 feet, cable. Spiced them together.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You had 200 on the stump puller?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: No, 200 foot cable. I had to dig three quarters of a mile through a field.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I'll show you that field over here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, you sure?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Three quarters of a mile, we had to dig every 400 feet.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Dead man log, 10 foot long, three foot and hard pin.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: It's an anchor, don't you see?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: You didn't have to fill a hole up, but you had to have that much, that deep in order to hold it. We never did let one slip. We always had plenty of anchors.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did them old stump pullers work?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: You had a team with a sweep and they pull right around, wind the cable right around your stump puller. When you hooked the one end of the cable over there to your building, she started coming, oh, was I feeling good. Didn't move fast, but that took us about 10 short days. We were 10 short days because we only put in about 5 -6 hours a day because we both had a lot of chores late in the fall, in November, cold weather, which I was glad of. I plowed this field, I was pulling right through this field that I'm going to show you, three quarters of a mile, and I was just wishing I could get through it before bad weather because that would have been a mess. Well, we did. We got it right down on some orchard grass, right by the pond, pretty close to where I wanted to set the house.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I told my neighbor then, I said, golly, I can handle it now. I said, because I knew there would be a lot of work to it, but that's all I had to do, all the weather, chores, and that was my job. The reason I wanted to close the pond was so I could have handy to water for the salad. Dad had a good pond, but he didn't have a good nut to suit me one winter. There's another thing I want to tell you. One winter, when we had the cattle, my little brother, Joe, was with me. We had to drive our cattle three miles to get to the water.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: That little pond that Dad had froze over, solid.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: But Rogers had a great big pond, we drove our cattle down there. Once a day is all you could afford to do is cold weather, and they'd drink good once a day and then fed them straw seed. So I said, Joe, next spring we're going to fix that pond. I said, I think I got an idea. You see, you had to dig down a ditch and fill it with clay to make it hold. I said, the water back in the field, but I said, I don't care, I want water. I said, I want this kind of a job no more, which I did. I reed the bank up, see. I forget now, three or four feet.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Just with clay? Just pile of clay?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Well, no, first clay next to the bank, for the water strip at, you see. About three foot, three or four foot wide bank. That'll hold the water. And then finish filling, you could, if you wanted, had to have to fill your bank.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What time of the year did you get all this?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Oh, this was, we done that late in the fall when the pond was practically dry, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did Dad do it?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: You betcha. Instead of three feet of water in dry weather, I had seven or eight feet of water. Nice, clear water. Dad was smart enough to have a pipe, to pipe it into a tank outside.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: But then we had another pipe, from that down to the hog lot, with floats in, don't you see?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And in Thurman, I didn't have to bother with the water for the sows after they got it, their little pigs got weaned when they got outside.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: They had the water out there. All I had to do was feed them their grain.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did this clay come from right on the place there?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Oh yeah, we just dug down, dug off black dirt down the clay and then dug it out. It worked, but by God we did it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you use, did you just do shovels?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Oh good, we had a team and what they call a slip. That's a two horse thing that, then I think even, no I don't know if we had the, you see they do have a what they call a fresnel four horse scraper.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: But you had a two horse. We had a two horse, a little handier.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: You could handle it with two horses. You can do a lot of work with one of them, a couple of guys can.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And you can get in narrower places, don't you see?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And that's all you had to have, a little there on the bottom, well about as wide as that, three feet, something like that. And it's all one team can do to pull, and when you fill it, pull. Fill that up. I mean I got that all fixed up. Well then one year.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: The year after I got this house put in, I had these fourteen sows, fourteen petitions off in this house.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I think you told me about how the house made good then. Yeah, yeah, yeah. Well maybe you don't want that. Well there's one thing I do want, because I'll try to help you, we'll get the things you wouldn't help me.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But on moving that hog house, how did you get it on skid, was that a big job?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Oh, no we had to jack it up. Oh, jack it up, see. Enough so we could work under that. And I had, Dad had timber on the place, and I got a couple of big bold pine trees peeled.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Green ones, they were slick. But you see, you peel the bark off, and they would slip along easier. Oh you had to have, they'd bore off anyway, and we'd just peel, I was just peeling before we'd get them under there.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: God I was young then, I could work like a tiger. I was only, that was you see in 1920, wait a minute now, 23 and 4.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: See 23 I was what, 23, 35 years old. Man, the work didn't amount to anything for me. I just worked my tailbone off on that ranch, but I tell you I made a go of it.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Listen, what I'd like to do is, why don't we go back and kind of start at the beginning and work our way up to here, so I can see it all as a picture. The whole thing in... Yeah, that's what I wanted to do. Well why don't we start then with the, now I was gonna... Wait till I get this pop of dirt. Okay.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I always tell you when a person settles on the ridge.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Are you talking about Central Ridge?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Yeah, that's where we live. That's where Dad hopes to, Central Ridge.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And see, I was only 8 years old when Dad moved over there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He moved from Park, did he?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Yep, with a wagon and a high use team.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: A high use team.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Moved, we didn't get more than, we got over on Big Barrage first day. Next day I think we got to, as near as I can remember, either Cavendish or Peck. When we got to Peck, we stayed there. Well, he left us, all the family but me. I went with Dad. I was the oldest, but I was only 8 years old.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: He took me along anyway.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And, he says, God, there was no road up that mountain. You had to go right up that old ridge. The high uses couldn't pull a whole wagon, so he just played smart. That's why I say he's a good figurehead. He worked hard, struggled. Now, if he hadn't had a little head on him, he wouldn't have done all that. What he done, he put a pole in this hind wheels. You know, that makes them just like one cart. Hooked his horses on there and all he piled on there was just the cook stove and some dishes. Box of dishes and cook stove. We got to going up this mountain. There was a dog gone steep one place they couldn't pull it, see. Well, Dad unloaded the dishes. And I guess he finally made them pull the stove a little ways, and then he went back and carried the dishes up. I was too little to help him. 8 years old, you know. But he done all the work and I seen him do it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there even a track for him to follow up the hillside?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: No, we got up the hill. Well, there's a kind of what they call a saddle after you got about darn two -thirds of the way up the mountain.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Kind of a level spot, see. We rested there pretty good.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No trail or nothing up there?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Oh, nothing.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Just a mountain side. Just a hill grade.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I mean, we call it, I don't know what you would call it, kind of a ridge.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: They did build a grade later on, see. Farmers got together and cooperated and worked together and shoveled and picked and maybe they had scrapers. I suppose they did because they had quite a grade. They made about a three, four mile grade, see, down, made switchbacks on that grade.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: That's the old grade.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: So you rested two -thirds of the way up and then he got... Well, and when we got home, I was going to tell you that. When we got home, he had a little cab on the homestead that he had... He'd been over here and homesteaded, filed on it, see. You see, he had a little homestead in Park, above where Granddad's place, where Steiner lives now. But he could relinquish them days. You could what they call relinquish. And he played smart. Oh, everybody in Park thought he was a fool, you know, didn't figure he could make it. But he was smart enough that he took this place, he laid it in a Z. He got an 80 land... wait, no, it was east and west here. Here's west. Yeah. He got an 80 land here, east and west. Then another one over here, east and west, that 40 joined... The east 40 joined this west 40, see. So it laid in a Z. Well, this neighbor's place that he bought, as I tell you that I read the beans on, was the 30 or 40 acres that laid in between this... in the corner of this Z.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Filled his place, I'll see.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Besides, made a lot of pasture land down in the canyon. That place had 150, 60 acres on it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, why did he pick the east -west? Did that give him all ridge?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Well, it was the best land with a land laid in between this draw and the canyon.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So it was all on the ridge, more or less on the ridge?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: It's a ridge, that's a ridge. It's called Central Ridge. That's why they had the... So if he'd have took it the other way, he would have gotten canyon, and he didn't know. Oh, he wouldn't have gotten near the land that he got. Oh, you betcha. He played smart, taking it like he did. Okay. And, in a way, he had this little cabin built from these timber that was on there, you see, just a pine log. I think it was about 11 by 12, something like that, and he didn't even have a door.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: He had just a hole that jumped in and out of just the dirt floor, and he had some sourdough that he had made, was all ready to go, see. Just a minute, Audrey.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: He'd made. I mean, he had left that.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I guess sourdough you can leave for quite a while. It's still good, see.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And he had a frying pan. And the only thing else we had to eat was just some fat pork. He didn't fry it very good, but you know how when you get real good, you know, it tastes pretty good. And he's going to be smart, though. He says, now look, he says, I'm going to show you how I can plop them. He didn't play foxy. He'd throw it up like that and melt it and just pound it down the dirt, but we just broke it off. It was good. Tasted good. I can remember that just like it happened yesterday.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: There it was, just a little eight -year -old kid, see.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Well, another little job he had me doing, and after he got established,

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: he scratched up 12 acres of ground. He barred a neighbor's horse, so he had three on a little 14 -inch plow.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Scratched just 12 acres up with.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And he butchered a cow long in the winter.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I guess he didn't have hay. He had to butcher and kept part of the meat, just part of it, and he traded the rest for some seed to sow this ground the next spring.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And the next year, he sold hay off of this piece of ground that he plowed up to help them along.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: You know how much he made in Harvest Field when he went out?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: He had to leave all of us there, and no water at home. There wasn't no water on the place.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And he left a horse there for us with a couple of syrup kegs on each side of him. We had to go down in the canyon where there was a spring to pack water at.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And us little barefooted boys, that was our summer's job.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And so when he'd come home, of course, he'd bought some shoes for us. I guess, I think a dollar, dollar and a half pair of them days. No one of us kids, you know, we'd get out and try to lose this guy.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: He'd come out there, moonlight night, say, you boys get in the house and take them shoes off. You know, tear them around, you save them for winter. It's just that hard up.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage

This uncle, Uncle Helle, he had a nice place. That was really the best place in Park, almost. Laid a lot of it level, but it was a little bit on the wet side, swampy. It was nice from the level. And you see whoever come in there first, their choice, you know what I'm saying, on them homesteads, just like they did on the ridge. I heard Ed Swenson told me that there was one guy they called the Land Grabber. There was one guy there that took a lot of good land in the bottom. Well, I don't know who that would have been at the time, let's see. Oh, it doesn't matter. Well, I'll tell you, they sold out, but they couldn't homestead more than one quarter, you know. Oh, no, that's just a small place compared to, well, you know, the central ridge. When you get up towards Nez Perce, there's all that crag -mont country. That spreads out wider. words: Well, I thought here's a good chance to drive four horses. If it wasn't hooked onto anything, I'd hitch them up, get them watered, hitch them up.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I'd practice pulling the lines this horse and that horse. Pretty soon I got confidence in myself. I told Dad, I says, you can get a gang plow now. I said, I can drive four horses.

words: That place. He had to fence that place all by himself. Make his wood as timber, and post. That's what I told Tyler. He thinks he's doing something fencing. speaker: Helmer Ringsage

speaker: Helmer Ringsage

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Yeah, that's the first year. I had good luck. 14 showers brought me 109 pigs. Now, you told me. Yeah, well, I saved them.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I mean, I saved them. I saved 105, but I had to borrow $500 to feed them over to my grain trash.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I guess, did I tell you that a door once?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Yeah, yeah, you told me that. Well, then you got that.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Well, that was it. And then, the next year, I didn't have good luck. But, as I said, that last year, that's when I borrowed that. You see, on summer follow, I had half corn, half peas. And I would hog the peas first, because they'd come on first. Little Alaskans. And as soon as they were done, by that time, the corn would be plenty ripe and the pigs would go right into it. And by then, the place, the fence hog dug, I'd just turn the hog loose. The only thing, they had to come over to the pond to get their water, but that's all they had to do.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Go over back and fill up and come and, maybe in the middle of the day, when it was hot, they'd come and drink up. And then they'd go along in the evening, go out and fill up again.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Well, hell, it worked slick. And so that year, as I said, though, I didn't have quite as many. I only had, instead of 105, I saved, I think it was about 85 pigs.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Because I let the fruit sows run with the fattening hogs, and they got too fat, so they didn't have good luck with their pigs. But I did save that many, and the price come up. See, the first year that I barred the 500, I only got six, six and a half cents a pound.

speaker: Sam Schrager

words: The next year, the price come up. First half, I sold, I got eight and a half. Well, that brought me 44, I mean, put me out of debt.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I was in debt $4 ,400. Besides, I had a little money left to live on, and I told that little banker, and all, said, oh, you're just getting good started. They figured I was doing fine. I'd paid the bank up, you see. And I was able to pay that up, and I said, I want to get out. I've been there seven years struggling like mad. I was just about peedled out. I didn't weigh over 130, 140 pounds. I'll tell you why. The mister was sick that spring.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I had the summer fall, about last summer fall I was telling you about, that I barred, I wouldn't have got anything off of that ground. I didn't get a planter. It plowed early.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: It dried up in May. And this west, well, his name is Wesley Thomas, John, I call him, that theory. He was living on his dad's place, or his uncle's place, Wesley Thomas, was his uncle's name.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And he had this steel roller packer. And I got the idea then that God and this ground was so bad, plowed a great big chunk, and I had a double disc, seven horses on it. That's what I had to farm with so I could plow the ground when it was dry, see.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And that would chop the clods up, but it didn't pack it the way I wanted it, because I wanted to put peas in, and you've got to have ground in good shape for peas, and corn. And it was so dry, along about the 12th of May.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And I got over to Thomas, I said, how about hiring your roll?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Go ahead and take it, he says, I'm through with it. See, he had got his crop all in.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: So I went and used it, and I just rolled it and packed it, and then I'd disc it and harrow it and pack it again.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And I just had that doggone ground just packed and worked up pretty good, near the best I could. Then I planted my peas and my corn. It took till the 42 life or any of that come up, and I thought, well, gee whiz, if I get something, I'll be lucky. No rain! But there was moisture in the bottom. That land was rich. And in the winter, it would absorb this subsoil below, would hold it, if there's enough moisture in the winter to go down. You see, I hadn't plowed, no, by God, that's something I hadn't plowed before. I generally did plow my ground in the fall. But I hadn't come around to it. But you let go through spring. I was too busy, for some reason, or froze up or something. And there that spring, I had two mares going to fold, and the wife was sick of that. She brought birth to a dead baby boy, 10 pounds. Putting her diet with him.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: The name of.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So it didn't work out as being any better?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: No, that didn't pan out at all. I had to quit, and I went harvesting. I tell you, I worked for Jeff Stanford, the next one, in harvest. I got two and a half a day there, and bored. For 12 hours work, and he had a header. And I drove header box.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: So then, I stayed there all summer. Well, Dad, he was pretty good. That learned him a good lesson. Now, he got smart to help Steiner.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Steiner stayed home. So he told him, and he told me, he wanted this cash that I had made, because I didn't have enough to go to Canada on. And Dad would hard run.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And he said, now he says, I'll sell you boys a cow apiece, and you get the offspring, and you'll just be increasing that one.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Well, we agreed.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: We stayed there and worked. Kept getting cattle ahead, see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mean you gave up the idea of going?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Yeah, I gave up because I couldn't make money enough to go up there to Canada. And Dad fixed it that way. He kind of wanted to settle it down. He guess he figured that he didn't like the lettuce. Let me go clear up there and try to struggle in that cold country, I suppose.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: That's what he figured.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: So we stayed with that.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: That was 1906.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And you, uh... And then, wait. 1906.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Let's see, what was it?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: 1907. Dad took a notion. He was studying. He'd learned about it.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: In Moscow, and he could get to go to school, finish up his high school at the university, which he did.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Now, he passed his high school at Moscow University.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: He's a good -looking boy there.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Now, here's Dad. See, 1908 was. First, I'll have to tell you, about 19...

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Yeah, I don't... Let's see, 1907. Yeah, it was 1907, I guess, that Dad was building the house. I'll show you after a while. He got the carpenters. He had this fellow that he bought oats from up on the ridge.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: They were Fredericksen. They were good carpenters. And Dad, then they didn't have to pay, oh, $2 ,000 a day. It was a big wage. And he was hard running. He only had a small farm, 40 acres up there on the ridge.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: So he was glad to get some work, you know. But he was a good carpenter, and Dad knew it. And Dad got him to build his house and also the barn.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: In 1908, they had got the barn built, and that's when I wanted to tell you. Here he is. He took off to go to this school.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Wait, now, I'll find him pretty soon.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Here he is. That's my Dad.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Which school is that?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Well, that's Weltner School. That's chiropractor. He got the idea. He wanted to take that up. And he did. He passed on it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where did you go to school?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Fair back to Missouri.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And he had rented a place to Stein.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Stein and Evani was 1908. I was, uh, wait a minute, I was 21 then.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Yeah.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: 12 years on the 8th. Yeah, I was 20. Stein was 18. And, uh, but I just, I didn't want to rent it, people. I didn't know why Dad wanted to rent it to him, but he did.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And, uh, he went back to Missouri. He went back to Missouri, left us boys there. And, uh, I wanted to keep working out whenever I had time. I helped Stein on the farm while he had a boy there named Lee Galloway to help him plow. Them days, I think, then the, let's see, 1918. Well, we had, uh, 1908. We had gang plow. I don't know, maybe, oh, I know.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: He was running a gang plow and a walking plow.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: That's what he was doing. That's what he was doing, because I knew we had gang plow and there were lots of horses.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was your father's interest in chiropractors?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Well, because he thought that he could make, being a, as boys, big enough to do the farming, and he'd go out and do little stuff, which he did. He went over to Kendrick one year and practiced.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And then, when I started, when he rented out to me, after I was married, you know, 1917, then he pulled it, well, he went to Kirkland, Washington, bought a house and lot there.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And the grandparents stayed with us that summer, till fall.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And, because Uncle Ed, let's see, was he sold out?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Yeah. See, Uncle Ed had already sold out.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: So, the grandparents had to, that was the trick that Ed played on Dad.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: He first got Dad the promise to take care of the old parents for $1200, and yet Dad had helped him get that place over there. He furnished, I mean, he bought this property over here to park for $2200, Dad did, to give him a down payment on that place.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And then, Ed was lucky. That last year that he farmed, he got $2000 worth of beans off the place, and he got $8000 from the place, and it only cost him $5000 to begin with.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: He got reckless with that money and he went down to southern Idaho, where my aunt, his sister, lived, and Albert Stanford, and started in with some cattle, but they couldn't make it.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: It was a dry country, so he didn't stay there long.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: He just let it go, and then he got the bright idea to go to camp.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now, is that the one that killed his wife?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: That's the one, yeah. Yeah, he killed his wife.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: He finally shot her with a rifle, and then he took his own life in the night.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you hear? Did you hear about what happened?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: No, the people, the relatives told me, Uncle Jim, they found out, you know, news scatters, you know. Yeah, that goes like wildfire, something like that, you know. Yes, he was jealous of her.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: She had to go out with the neighbor lady in a buggy. And they were all heaps of nugs.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I knew something wrong with the old bastard when I was there, but I was kind of a bully sort of a guy, French -Canadian, you know. They were pretty hot -headed.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And so I just, I was glad to get out of there. Geez, I got over there to Swanson's. They were nice people.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was he mean when you worked with him?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: No, he didn't order me around any. He didn't do that. And he seemed that that was when they first married.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: He was pretty sweet to each other then, don't you see?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: But this was, I don't know, a year or two years after when he shot her, that they got on the out with each other for some reason.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: He got jealous of her going with the neighbors, women, talking. I don't know, maybe someone was, pretty mouth -piercing.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And he'd overheard something that made him mad, don't you see? But the other thing I was going to ask you is, now did your father ever, did he do much practicing of chiropractic?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Yeah, he did. You see, he went, he done a little in Idaho, in Kendrick, but when he got out to Kirkland Martin, he had to take different licenses out there.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: But he practiced out there, too.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: You betcha. He had a lot of time. He was, well, like I said, I farmed his place seven years.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And he was practicing all that?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Yeah, he was practicing what he could. And Granddad, he had him there then, and before Grandma died, that was before 1922, that he, Granddad was cutting wood for him.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: They had wood around there, you see.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And I guess he had a fireplace or something that he was feeding the neighbors. He might have been cutting wood, but I don't remember that. So he believed in chiropractors. Oh yeah, I tell you, he's helped out.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Now I tell you, one time he did help me out.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I was, let's see, that was after he'd done that, but that was before I got married. I was helping him haul alfalfa hay in.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: This barn, he had had hay shoots to shove the hay down, you know, down to the feed walkway, feedway.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And I was up in the mile, Dad was down there unloading with slings. We had slings, two or three slings to a load of hay. And I was up there placing the hay around and telling him when to strip it. Well, I stepped back like that, didn't see, you know, in a hurry. There was a little loose hay on this shooting up there with my head, I mean, ass down.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Lit right on my head. Come on. Put me down there, Jesus. Land all my weight right on my tailbone and the bone walked.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Dad hollered at me, said, what's the matter? He said, you up in the mile? What's going on?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I said, oh Dad, I fell down the hay shoot and I said, I can't get up. By God, he come in there and he treated me for about a half an hour on my back and down my tailbone. By God, he took me out of it.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And I learned from then on that that was quite a bit to it, that nerve. He treated the nerves, see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did he do? Did he massage you down there?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Massage me, God. And so it hadn't been for him and I never did get over it right away, but it started me. It took me out of that shock, see. Him knowing something about nerve treatments. It took me out of that shock and I went right back toward him. Only half an hour that he had to treat me until I was right back up there placing the hay. Finished holding his hay. Another thing, I didn't tell anyone, well maybe we better leave that until next time.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: When he went to eat to get to school, that's when we leave off. Then I painted the barns because he had the carpenters leave those castles up there.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: We'll start on that. I had the targets and girls and her name was Emma Gullingford.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And she was good, just followed by the Norwegians. She could talk Norwegian good, but I couldn't. I could understand it, see. Because I always talked to Emma in Norwegian, so I could talk it plenty good so we could understand one another. She wanted to be dead, but she liked the coffee. She thought I'd have it. I just didn't cooperate, but she needed some.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: See, I had a lot of chances when I was young, you know. But anyway, we were dancing away because this was good music. We danced from one room into the other. That's how we danced.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Small room, see, probably as big as this. And you'd just dance from one room all around in this and then you'd move into the other. The whole crowd did the same thing. It was just a lot of fun. And these, Fritz Olson and Willie Osborn, they were playing violin.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And I think, I forget now whether it was a chordine or a harp of some kind that Fritz was playing for second music.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Oh, you just couldn't sass it. You had a waltz, they'd just pull you around, you know. When them old timers played them waltz, I tell you, that really moved your legs if you had any waltz in you.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And we'd learned, Steiner and I had learned dancing on the ridge when we was kids. See, my aunt used to cook for Dad after Mother's Day.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: We were just kids, but all the neighbor women over there would learn, would love to dance with us kids just because they felt sorry for us.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: We were, you might say orphans, no mother, so they'd never turn us down. We'd go and ask them and they'd just say, well, one lady especially, Mrs. Malcolm, her husband didn't even dance on the floor for some reason. And she was a good dancer. I always got her to dance. And it was the McBride.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This was, were these dances in homes on Central Ridge too? On Central Ridge, were they having dances in people's homes too?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Yeah, that's where it was. Just like around Deary. Just like around Deary. I said, that's where we learned. Yeah. Yeah, we'd go from one neighborhood to the other and once in a while we'd go clear up what they call, Walworth. That was a little country town, you know, between Nez Perce and Craigmont. It was kind of a little bit north. And this test support, this Dutch kid, you know, he said, come on up, Helmer. He said, sometime we'll go out there, they're going to have a mass parade. He didn't have mass parade dancers.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: So he can tell who they were.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Oh, God, we had a hell of a good time that night, see. And there's all kinds of young people there. God, and he had a sister, you know, Laura. I'd dance with her and all the rest of them. And they all mixed. God, there was no high -hat going on. Everybody got busy and enjoyed themselves.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: See what I mean?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Just like we did at Deary. We did that too. I used to, when we lived in Park, we used to go over to Cedar Creek once in a while. And the Cedar Creek folks would come to Park, dance in our schoolhouse there.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: That's what we did there. And over on Cedar Creek, there was Smith's.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Did you ever hear the Smith's over on Cedar Creek?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I think I heard that name.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: It used to be Ben Smith and George Smith were the two boys. And Eva Smith, she's the one that became the owner of the home place.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: They lived just across from the Longfellow place, if you remember where the Longfellow place was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So she used to dance with them?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Well, we danced in Smith's house. They had a big house.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And she was a good dancer. She was a big woman, but by God, she was easy on her feet. Oh God, I could dance waltzes with her just like nothing. Just finding dandy.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: She finally moved down to either Clarkson or Lewiston and became a nurse, I guess.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This masquerade dance, everybody got dressed up until they were born?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Well, yeah, that was the masquerade over there. No, I'm thinking of that one. But the masquerade, yeah, that was. Oh, you bet. That was lots of fun. We just tore around together, you know. And of course, after lunchtime, then they'd fill the masquerade off.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: You wouldn't know who the hell you had for a partner, see?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I think sometimes you'd be buying baskets too, you know. Basket socials and so on.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You bid on it?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Yeah, you bid on it. You wouldn't know who your partner was until you got the mask off. Sometimes you'd get what you wanted, sometimes you wouldn't.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Then you ate your lunch with the person that fixed it. Oh yeah, if you bought a basket from somebody, you had to eat your lunch with that person, regardless. There's no lady, that didn't matter. You'd eat dinner with her anyway. But there wasn't many old ladies out there, dancers. Most of the young ones, they're mad ladies, but that was all right.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How long did these dances go? Did they go late into the night?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Oh yeah, sometimes by God we wouldn't get home until daylight. You'd take the Fourth of July especially, we'd go to Peck, Idaho. And then we didn't come home until daylight, you know.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: But we were trying to sleep in the daytime, you'd hear that. Oh, I let it go, you couldn't go to sleep.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You were trying to sleep after the dance?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Yeah, after the dance, at home in daylight.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: You just couldn't talk good.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And old Dad, you know, uh oh, when we was down in the ridge, just learning.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Next morning, you know, after we'd been dancing, we'd be in the bed maybe a couple of hours, just nice sound of sleep. Dad would come up and holler, come on boys, get your chores done.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: The way we snuck out, see Auntie told us, Dad didn't want us to go to the dance. She said, we'll put a ladder up here. She was two years old now, I see. Put a ladder up upstairs where we walked up.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That was some of the first dancing you done?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Yeah, that was when we was first learning. Oh, I guess we were little devils, but thank God, I mean, I went through it all.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And I'm glad I didn't get a kick out of it, I'm thinking about it. What a hell of a good time I have had in my younger days.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: These lunches, by the way, they had real good feeds. Oh, they had cakes and donuts, and sometimes they'd have some cooked beans or something like that, you know.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: What do you call them, baked beans?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Oh, you bet. They were really fat. Country people, you know, they could bring on them old farm ladies. They could cook good, I tell you. They knew what a farm boy eat. They could have bought a plant too. The whole family would go. Oh, no, no. No, well, Mal Malcolm, his children were too small, see, so it'd just be him and his wife. And them boys were over there. That man started out there. There was a nice, nice company. He come to that ridge, he didn't homestead. He had to buy the land. He bought a quarter, no, 80 acres. An old man McKay, and then he bought Kay McKay's 80. There was two boys and the old man, three 80s. Then when he got able, he bought the other man's 80.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: That was Dennis McKay.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Well, Rod Pfeiffer had bought one of them 80s, see. Well, later on then, he got... Oh, Malcolm was a prosperous guy. He raised his own horses. He had a stallion of his own. And he was pretty prosperous. He was a good farmer. And his wife, a hard worker, and they got up early. They had long ways to haul the market, see.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: They were five years, five miles up the ridge, and then five miles to Peck from there. They had a ten -mile haul when they all grew.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: But anyway, old Malcolm, he had this money ready for this Pfeiffer place, and he bought that. That made it 248. And he had my brothers, Julius and Simon, helping him plow. He had two six -horse teams, gang plows, black perchings. And he hired them boys. Them days, dollar a day, it was big money.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Dollar a day in both.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: They plowed and took care of the horses the best they could. And they became good friends with Malcolm's, and he'd hire them every spring, see.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: To help plow, because he could depend on Simon, especially.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: The only trouble with Simon was the leafy head. He was hard to wake up in the morning.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: But Julius wasn't. Julius had come right up.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: So that's the way it went, and later years after we moved away, they kept... Well, his boys grew up too. He had two boys and a daughter. And she married a farmer over on Angel Ridge that had a farm. So she was gone, but the two boys stayed, and the old folks, they just kept building up. The boys helped, helped, helped. And they got into this modern machinery, and they could start grabbing more land, you see. You know, if you got one section of land, or half a section, the farm wouldn't... You buy another section, half a section, this and that makes two sections.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: You could pay it lots quicker, and he was smart enough to figure that out.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And that's the way he kept going. Today them boys are farming, the last I heard, they were farming 1 ,600 acres. And of course, they got a lot of grandchildren. They're all grown up, and now it's the grandchildren taking over. See, the oldest boys, well, he's around, now see, the youngest boys are around 65. He's as young as I am, quite a bit, about 20 -some years. But the oldest boy, he's little older, he's about, I guess about 70. It looks to me like that clock. Yeah, it's one o 'clock. It looks like it's stopping. Oh, quarter after one, yeah it is, I haven't warmed it. I've got to wind it.
